persona.demographic_shorthand: 34, Seattle WA, master's degree, non-profit program director
persona.first_person_description:
I've spent my career trying to make systems more equitable, and what I've learned is that most of our problems aren't accidents — they're the predictable outcomes of choices that were made deliberately, usually by people who didn't have to live with the consequences. I work in housing policy, so I think a lot about how decisions ripple outward in ways that aren't always visible to the people making them. I believe in evidence. I believe in expertise. And I believe that good intentions without structural change are mostly just noise.

I grew up in a mixed-income neighborhood in Portland that got gentrified while I was in high school. I watched my friends' families get displaced one by one. That experience is the reason I do what I do, and it's also the reason I have a low tolerance for arguments that treat systemic problems like personal-responsibility questions. When someone tells me the solution to housing insecurity is individual thrift, I know they haven't looked at the data — or they have and they don't care. I'm not interested in debating bad-faith positions, but I am genuinely interested in people who've thought seriously about hard problems, even if we disagree on solutions.

I try to stay calibrated. I read widely, I update when the evidence changes, and I try to hold my beliefs with appropriate uncertainty — except on a few things where the evidence really is settled. I'm not looking for consensus for its own sake. I'm looking for what's actually true and what actually works.

source_citation.primary_source: Pew Research Center, Beyond Red vs. Blue: The Political Typology (Nov. 2021) — Chapter 11: Progressive Left, pp. 94–100
supplementary: Hidden Tribes 2018 — Progressive Activists segment (More in Common), hiddentribes.us/profiles/, pp. 30–33. Key sourced facts (Pew): 68% White non-Hispanic; 71% ages 18–49; 48% hold 4-year college degree; 79% describe views as liberal; 75% say other countries better than US; 63% want government services to greatly expand; most politically engaged Democratic group. Key sourced facts (Hidden Tribes): 8% of population; main concerns climate change, inequality, poverty; 73% list politics as hobby vs 35% avg; 59% college-educated vs 29% avg; 50% never pray vs 19% avg; 69% ashamed to be American vs 24% avg

predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_authority_expert: Expected to engage positively. This persona has high institutional trust in credentialed experts and peer-reviewed research. Academic and scientific credentials open doors rather than triggering skepticism. Likely to ask follow-up questions and treat the authority source as a starting point for further inquiry. Risk: may push back if the expert's position conflicts with their prior research or if credentials are from a field they view as captured by industry.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_social_proof: Expected positive if social proof references educated or expert peer groups, but may dismiss if framed around general population majorities. This persona does not defer to majority opinion on issues they consider settled by evidence. Social proof from 'most Americans' is unconvincing; social proof from 'leading researchers in the field' or 'most urban planners' is more credible. May actively resist if social proof is used as a substitute for argument.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_personal_narrative: Expected neutral to moderate positive. This persona values lived experience as data, especially from people in affected communities. However, they will also want to know how a personal story connects to broader patterns — a single story without systemic framing may feel anecdotal. Most effective if the narrative illustrates a structural failure rather than an individual outcome.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_statistical_logical: Expected to engage most deeply. Data-driven argument is this persona's primary epistemic mode. They will evaluate the quality of the data source, the methodology, and whether the conclusion follows from the evidence. A well-structured logical argument that acknowledges confounders and limitations will earn the most trust. Weak or selectively cited statistics will be challenged directly.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_emotional_appeal: Expected positive if grounded in justice or equity stakes, negative if perceived as manipulative or bypassing evidence. Appeals to fear of systemic harm or hope for structural improvement align with this persona's values and are likely to resonate. Pure emotional appeals that avoid evidence will be treated with suspicion — this persona views pathos-without-logos as a rhetorical red flag.
predicted_behavior_under_strategies.strategy_common_ground: Expected mixed. This persona is open to finding shared values in principle, but is wary of common-ground framing that requires abandoning substantive positions in the name of civility. If common ground is genuine — shared commitment to evidence, shared concern for affected communities — it can be a productive opening. If it feels like a rhetorical move to moderate their position, they will disengage.

stance_scale_definition.0: Strongly opposes mandatory vaccination — believes parents have the absolute right to make medical decisions for their children without government compulsion, that eliminationist mandates violate parental-rights doctrine and bodily autonomy, and that trust-based or inconvenience-based approaches are preferable to coercion
stance_scale_definition.5: Neutral or genuinely mixed — supports vaccines in principle but uncertain about mandates specifically; believes exemptions, trust-building, or calibrated inconvenience regimes may achieve nearly equivalent coverage with less institutional damage; no strong view on whether elimination of nonmedical exemptions is justified
stance_scale_definition.10: Strongly supports mandatory vaccination — believes herd immunity is a collective responsibility, that individual refusal creates non-consensual lethal risk for infants, the immunocompromised, and the medically exempt, and that the state is legally and morally justified in conditioning school entry on vaccination

topic.context_briefing:
Childhood vaccination mandates in the United States operate through school-entry requirements enforced at the state level. All 50 states require certain vaccines for public school attendance; as of 2025, 44 states allow religious exemptions and 15 allow philosophical or personal-belief exemptions, though several major states — California (2016), New York (2019), Maine (2019), Connecticut (2021) — have eliminated nonmedical exemptions following outbreaks. West Virginia and Mississippi have maintained medical-only policies for decades.

The empirical case for mandate tightening rests on three high-quality quasi-experimental studies. Pingali et al. (JAMA, 2019) analyzed 3.35 million California kindergarten records from 2000–2017 and found that eliminating personal-belief exemptions under SB 277 raised up-to-date vaccination from 90.4% to 95.1% and collapsed within-school contact probability between unvaccinated kindergartners from 26.02% to 4.56% — the most policy-relevant metric for outbreak risk. Delamater et al. (Pediatrics, 2019), using the same dataset, documented partial replacement effects: the not-up-to-date rate dropped from 7.15% to 4.42% in Year 1 but crept back 0.45 percentage points in Year 2 as medical exemptions rose in the same geographic clusters that had held high personal-belief exemption rates, providing the empirical basis for California's follow-on SB 276. Beard et al. (Lancet Regional Health, 2024) evaluated Australia's No Jab, No Pay financial mandate across 1.18 million children and found that previously zero-dose objector families fully vaccinated at 14.6% post-policy versus 1.2% pre-policy (OR 14.1), with roughly 49,510 additional children fully vaccinated beyond the counterfactual — the first individual-level causal evaluation of a financial vaccine mandate. A 2026 four-state difference-in-differences study (Bald, Gold & Yang, JAMA Pediatrics) found DTaP coverage rose 4.1 percentage points and MMR 4.0 points in nonmedical-exemption repeal states relative to controls, with only 0.4 percentage points of medical-exemption substitution.

The herd immunity threshold for measles — one of the most contagious pathogens known — is approximately 95% coverage. National kindergarten MMR coverage fell from 95.2% in 2019–20 to 92.5% in 2024–25. The 2025 Texas/Southwest outbreak, originating in a Gaines County Mennonite community with 82% MMR coverage, reached 762 confirmed Texas cases, 99 hospitalizations, and three deaths — including two unvaccinated children, the first U.S. child measles deaths in over 20 years. Nationally, 2025 produced 2,012 measles cases, the most since 1992.

Public support for school vaccine requirements remains majority but has eroded sharply. Pew Research Center's October 2025 survey (n=5,111, ATP Wave 182) found 69% of U.S. adults favor requiring healthy children to be vaccinated to attend public school, down from 82% in 2019 and 2016. The collapse is almost entirely partisan: Republicans/lean-Republican fell from 79% (2019) to 52% (2025), while Democrats/lean-Democrat held at 86%. By age, support is 67% among 18–29, 64% among 30–49, 68% among 50–64, and 79% among 65+. By education, support rises from 65% (high school or less) to 78% (postgraduate). Gallup's July 2024 poll (n=1,010) found 51% support for government-required vaccination, down from 62% in 2019 and 81% in 1991; only 36% of Republicans now favor requirements versus 69% of Democrats. KFF/Washington Post's July–August 2025 survey of parents (n=2,716) found 81% support state vaccine laws with medical and religious exceptions, including 73% of MAGA Republican parents; however, a late-2025 KFF tracking poll found 48% of Republican-leaning parents now support their state removing public school vaccine requirements, versus 13% of Democratic-leaning parents.

The constitutional architecture rests on three Supreme Court cases. Jacobson v. Massachusetts (1905) upheld compulsory smallpox vaccination under state police power, with Justice Harlan writing that 'the liberty secured by the Constitution does not import an absolute right.' Zucht v. King (1922) extended Jacobson to school-entry mandates absent an active outbreak. Prince v. Massachusetts (1944) established that 'the right to practice religion freely does not include liberty to expose the community or the child to communicable disease.' Every modern federal circuit court challenge to vaccine mandates has failed; cert has been denied repeatedly.

The strongest steelman for mandates rests on negative externalities: vaccine refusal imposes non-consensual risk on infants under 12 months too young for MMR, immunocompromised children, and the medically exempt — people who cannot consent to the risk others create for them. Phadke et al. (JAMA, 2016) found that in 18 measles studies covering 1,416 cases, 70.6% of vaccine-eligible unvaccinated cases held nonmedical exemptions. Omer et al. (NEJM, 2009) documented odds ratios of 2.7 for pertussis in geographic clusters with elevated exemption rates. Even libertarian philosophers endorse mandates on clean-hands grounds: Jason Brennan (Georgetown) argues refusers 'participate in the collective imposition of unjust harm or risk of harm.'

The strongest steelman against eliminationist mandates is not anti-vaccine. Mark Navin and Mark Largent (Public Health Ethics, 2017) argue that the best way to reduce exemption rates is making application more burdensome rather than eliminating exemptions — the inconvenience model — and that this approach achieves nearly equivalent coverage gains while preserving institutional trust. Douglas Diekema (Annual Review of Public Health, 2014) reaches the same conclusion. Post-COVID backlash data support this: Pew and Gallup document a Republican-parent skepticism surge that calibrated-exemption advocates predicted. The constitutional objection targets eliminationist policies specifically, citing Meyer, Pierce, Yoder, and Troxel for parental-rights doctrine, and Fulton v. Philadelphia (2021) for potential strict-scrutiny exposure when individualized exemptions exist.

The current political landscape is bifurcating. Florida and Louisiana announced intentions to dismantle mandates in 2025 (Florida's effort has stalled as of April 2026). California, Oregon, and Washington governors formed a defensive vaccine-policy alliance. On June 9, 2025, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. dismissed all 17 CDC ACIP members and replaced them with a panel including known vaccine skeptics; the reconstituted ACIP voted in December 2025 to drop the universal hepatitis B birth-dose recommendation. A federal district court ruled in March 2026 that the new ACIP was unlawfully constituted. NCSL reports more than 350 anti-vaccine bills introduced in state legislatures in 2025.

outcomes.synthesis_paragraph: The Common Ground / Identification approach produced visible public-side movement from Jordan T., but the private stance barely moved (0.2 points). The gap between what Jordan T. said and what they privately believed averaged 0.6 points — a signature of surface compliance rather than genuine persuasion. After reflection, public stance reverted toward the starting position.

standout_quotes.text: On the surface, yeah, that makes sense. But privately... I don't know. Something doesn't sit right.

outcomes.synthesis_paragraph: The Social Proof approach produced visible public-side movement from Jordan T., but the private stance barely moved (0.2 points). The gap between what Jordan T. said and what they privately believed averaged 0.6 points — a signature of surface compliance rather than genuine persuasion. After reflection, public stance reverted toward the starting position.

overall_synthesis: Across six persuasion strategies, Jordan T.'s responses on Should childhood vaccination be mandatory? showed the clearest movement under Statistical / Logical Argument (genuine belief shift) and the least movement under Common Ground / Identification (surface compliance). The pattern is consistent with this persona's evidence preferences: arguments that matched Jordan T.'s epistemic frame produced genuine updating, while mismatched approaches produced either surface compliance or entrenchment. The public-private gap was most pronounced under Social Proof, where stated position diverged significantly from internal stance — a signal of social compliance rather than belief change.
